meta.title: Leviathan — One dispatch loop. Every surface.
meta.description: How Poly, registry routing, DNA exec contracts, and @lev-os packages turn agent work into auditable execution—not another chat thread.

props.title: Ship agent work like infrastructure—not improvisation
props.subtitle: Poly is the registry-driven dispatch layer: daemons first, cold binaries when needed, typed runners for the long tail. DNA locks the exec contract so orchestration stays out of the hot path.

props.content: If your team is tired of “it worked in the demo thread,” you need the same thing production software always needed: **routing you can explain**, **handlers you can swap**, and **contracts that don’t silently fork**.

props.label: WHY THIS MATTERS
props.title: Chat is alignment. Execution is the product.
props.subtitle: Leviathan is built for teams who want receipts: declarative surfaces (CLI, MCP, HTTP, gRPC, and more), graph-aware work, and a harness that treats `lev exec` as first-class—not a bolt-on script.

props.content:
Most “agent platforms” optimize for the message UI. Leviathan optimizes for the **dispatch loop**: who is allowed to run, how overload is handled, what events get emitted, and how capability is described as data (cards, topology, manifests) instead of tribal knowledge.

**Poly** is the SDK-shaped entry to that loop: one `call(provider, method, ctx)` that resolves through a cached registry and lands on the right transport—HTTP daemons, local binaries, or registered `lev://` handlers—without turning your repo into a maze of one-off integrations.

props.title: 🔀 Registry-first routing
props.content: Load `registry-runtime.yaml` / `registry.yaml`, match **daemons** (HTTP/S when healthy), fall back to **binaries**, then **runners** keyed as `provider:method`. Same mental model as internal service discovery—because agents are services.

props.title: 📜 DNA-locked exec contract
props.content: `exec-contract` defines how `@lev-os/exec` behaves: bounded concurrency, backpressure, event sequences (`exec.started`, `exec.completed`, `exec.failed`), and a hard line—**no sneaking orchestration into exec**. Adapters stay honest; the control plane stays separate.

props.title: 🧭 Surfaces, not a default chat protocol
props.content: Domain types describe **capabilities** and **surfaces** (CLI, MCP, HTTP, gRPC, …). The product story is “route work to the right interface,” not “everything is a thread.” Chat overlays exist where they help; they don’t define the architecture.

props.label: UNDER THE HOOD
props.title: The Poly dispatch order (the part buyers audit)
props.subtitle: Fast path → cold path → codegen handler path.

items.title: Daemons
items.description: If a provider maps to a healthy HTTP/HTTPS daemon, call across the wire with executeHandler — same primitive the SDK uses for remote surfaces.

items.title: Binaries
items.description: On failure (or when configured), fall back to registered binaries — spawned processes with structured args, not shell improvisation.

items.title: Runners
items.description: Registry runners resolve provider:method to handlers — lev:// modules dynamically imported, or bin:// paths with manifest-mapped flags.

props.label: CLARITY
props.title: Two URI worlds—on purpose
props.subtitle: Strict route objects vs. filesystem-backed handler modules.

props.title: Route.parse (lev://module/fractal/item)
props.content: The domain **Route** type enforces a three-segment shape for structured addressing—great for graph-native links and tooling.

props.title: Handler executor (lev:// → import path)
props.content: The **codegen executor** resolves `lev://` to workspace-relative modules (`plugins/`, `core/`, `packages/`) and invokes `module[method](ctx)`. When you need both, document the hand-off—Leviathan’s codebase is honest that these segment rules differ today.

props.label: PLATFORM
props.title: Representative @lev-os packages (execution stack)
props.subtitle: A slice of the workspace—enough to see the spine: domain types, exec, poly, harness, graph, flowmind.

rows.role: Poly-protocol types, Route, ARM, shared vocabulary

props.content: ARM means **Agent Runtime Manifest** here—YAML manifests that normalize to capability cards—not CPU architecture. Naming is load-bearing; we say it plainly.

This page is a **sales letter**, not a spec: it translates repo-grounded behavior into buyer language. When you’re ready to go deeper, the same concepts appear as contracts (`dna/core/exec.dna.yaml`), types (`core/domain/src/poly-protocol/`), and the Poly SDK entrypoint (`core/poly/src/sdk/index.ts`).

**What you should do next:** run your own audit—trace one provider end-to-end, compare event logs to DNA expectations, and decide whether your organization wants chat-shaped demos or runtime-shaped shipping.

props.label: NEXT STEP
props.title: If execution is the product, prove it in your environment
props.subtitle: Fork the repo, wire one runner, emit one receipted `lev exec` path—then scale.

props.title: Evaluate Leviathan where it wins
props.content: Multi-surface agents, contract-first exec, registry routing, and graph-aware work—not a single-vendor chat lock-in. **Clone, integrate, measure.** If the dispatch story doesn’t hold up in your stack, you’ll know quickly—that’s the point.
